did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a novel AI tool named DermPathGPT01, built on the GPT-4 architecture, to analyze de-identified clinical photographs and patient context to generate differential diagnoses for cutaneous findings in post-allogeneic stem cell transplant patients. The diagnostic accuracy of this AI model was evaluated and benchmarked against dermatopathology results and board-certified dermatologist diagnoses.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is an abstract from the 67th ASH Annual Meeting presenting original experimental results. It describes a retrospective analysis of 56 clinical cases to evaluate the diagnostic accuracy of a novel AI tool (DermPathGPT01) against gold standards, reporting specific metrics such as top-5 accuracy (60.7%) and top-1 accuracy (41.1%).

title: Assessing diagnostic accuracy of DermPathGPT01 for dermatologic diagnosis in post-allogeneic stem cell transplant patients
brief_summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic accuracy of DermPathGPT01, a novel AI tool built on the GPT-4 architecture, for identifying cutaneous findings in post-allogeneic stem cell transplant patients. The retrospective analysis included 56 clinical cases derived from 51 patients who developed skin reactions at Montefiore Einstein between 2022 and 2024. The AI model analyzed de-identified clinical photographs and patient context to generate a top-5 ranked differential diagnosis, which was benchmarked against dermatopathology results or board-certified dermatologist diagnoses. DermPathGPT01 achieved a top-5 diagnostic accuracy of 60.7% and a top-1 accuracy of 41.1%. However, McNemar’s Test revealed that board-certified dermatologists significantly outperformed the AI in top-1 accuracy (p < 0.05). The authors conclude that while the model shows potential as a triage or decision support tool, it remains inferior to human experts and requires further refinement.

lesion_types_studied.lesion_type: Cutaneous complications of post-allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation
lesion_types_studied.details: The study evaluated a broad range of cutaneous findings in post-allogeneic stem cell transplant patients, arising from immune dysregulation, drug reactions, engraftment syndrome, graft-versus-host disease (GVHD), infections, and malignant infiltration. Specific lesion types or diagnostic classes were not enumerated in the provided text; the focus was on the diagnostic accuracy of the AI tool in generating differential diagnoses for these general categories.

all_task_types.details: The model generates a top-5 ranked differential diagnosis list for cutaneous findings in post-allogeneic stem cell transplant patients. The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic categories (e.g., GVHD, infections, drug reactions) based on clinical photographs and patient context. Performance was evaluated using top-1 and top-5 accuracy metrics.

datasets_used.name: Montefiore Einstein Post-Allogeneic HSCT Cohort
datasets_used.description: A retrospective cohort of patients who underwent allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (allo-HSCT) at Montefiore Einstein between January 2022 and December 2024. The dataset consists of de-identified clinical photographs and brief patient context (age, sex, transplant timing, medical history) for patients who developed skin reactions.
classes_used: Gold standard diagnoses (dermatopathology results or board-certified dermatologist diagnoses)

use.testing: The 56 cases were used to evaluate the diagnostic accuracy of DermPathGPT01. The AI's top-5 and top-1 predictions were benchmarked against gold standards (dermatopathology or dermatologist diagnoses).
use.other: The model (DermPathGPT01) was developed using this data context but the specific split for development vs. testing is not explicitly separated; the entire cohort of 56 cases was used for the reported accuracy evaluation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the diagnostic performance (specifically top-1 accuracy) between the AI model (DermPathGPT01) and board-certified dermatologists.
To assess inter-rater reliability (agreement) between the AI model and dermatologist diagnoses.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in top-1 diagnostic accuracy between DermPathGPT01 and board-certified dermatologists.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in top-1 diagnostic accuracy between DermPathGPT01 and board-certified dermatologists (specifically, dermatologists outperformed the AI).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: McNemar’s Test revealed that board-certified dermatologists significantly outperformed DermPathGPT01 in top-1 accuracy (p < 0.05).

board-certified dermatologists.comparison_note: Significantly outperformed DermPathGPT01 in top-1 accuracy (p < 0.05)

Montefiore Einstein Post-Allogeneic HSCT.notes: Cohen’s Kappa indicated no substantial agreement between AI and dermatologist diagnoses. McNemar’s Test revealed that dermatologists significantly outperformed DermPathGPT01 in top-1 accuracy (p < 0.05).

architecture_used.input_size: De-identified clinical photographs and brief patient context (age, sex, transplant timing, medical history)

preprocessing_techniques: Exclusion of patients without clinical photographs
conclusions: DermPathGPT01 demonstrated a strong ability to generate comprehensive differential diagnoses, achieving a top-5 diagnostic accuracy of 60.7%.

The first version of DermPathGPT01 remains inferior to board-certified dermatologists in selecting the most accurate diagnosis, with a top-1 accuracy of 41.1% and significantly lower performance (p < 0.05) in McNemar’s Test.
Cohen’s Kappa indicated no substantial agreement between the AI model and dermatologist diagnoses.
The model shows potential as a triage or decision support tool, particularly in settings with limited dermatology access.
Human expertise remains necessary for dermatologic diagnosis in this context.
Further refinement and validation of AI models across diverse skin types and hematologic settings is warranted to ensure equitability in clinical practice.
important_remarks: The study is a conference abstract (ASH 2025) with limited reporting depth; specific details on the GPT-4 version used and full statistical outputs (e.g., exact p-values, confidence intervals) are not provided.
The dataset is small (n=56 cases from 51 patients) and retrospective, limiting statistical power and generalizability.
The cohort is single-center (Montefiore Einstein), introducing potential population and dataset bias; external validation is lacking.
The study explicitly notes the need for further validation across diverse skin types to ensure equitability, indicating potential bias against non-Caucasian skin tones in the current model.

The AI model (DermPathGPT01) significantly underperformed board-certified dermatologists in top-1 accuracy (41.1% vs. superior dermatologist performance, p < 0.05), highlighting current limitations for standalone diagnostic use.

Gold standard definition had variability: diagnoses were counted as correct if in the top-3 when no specific diagnosis was favored, and dermatopathology reports without differentials were contextualized by dermatologist impressions, which may introduce subjectivity.
The model was not trained on this specific test set but evaluated on it; however, as a first version, it lacks evidence of robustness or iterative refinement in this specific clinical context.
